title: Binary, hex, casting and the modulo operation
_comment: See decks/W1C2.json for the format and the reasoning behind it.
Follows the retired LCS deck W1C3Digitalpracticeconv.pdf slide for
slide (its 23 slides, minus 3 of per-term announcements).
HOW THESE SLIDES WORK - do not 'improve' this by adding answers. An LCS
practice slide shows the PROBLEM STATEMENT ONLY. The class works it,
and Petra then presents the solution live. The handwriting visible on
top of the PDF pages is that live solution, not slide content. So a
'note' here may carry information the printed slide gave the students
up front (e.g. 'to help you, 177 -> 10110001'), but must never carry
the answer. Same for the discussion prompts: 'What are the ways to
represent negative numbers in binary?' is Petra asking the students to
explain what they read before class - it needs no note at all.
For the same reason this deck does not frame the book's worked examples
or its prose sections: that is the pre-class reading, and it gives the
answers away. Book pages appear only where the class works a real book
activity (the averaging one).
The casting and modulo slides are 'demo' slides: the LCS originals were
a line of framing plus one Dash coding window, which is what 'demo'
reproduces.
Several LCS pages are images in the PDF, so their text is not in its
text layer and had to come from Petra directly. Two of them (p11 and
p16) were bare section headers announcing a topic switch and are
deliberately dropped. The rest are the practice slides at 8, 10 and 12
below, two of which hand the students a blank table to fill in - see
the 'table' field and fillTable() in class.html.

slides.title: Practice
slides.body: Please convert the decimal number 177 to 8-bit binary.

slides.title: Practice
slides.body: Please convert the decimal number 177 to base-3.

slides.title: Practice: binary addition
slides.body: Add the decimal numbers 177 and 23 in 8-bit binary.
slides.note: To help you, here are the binary conversions: 177 → 10110001, 23 → 00010111

slides.title: Now try this one
slides.note: Recall that the binary representation of 177 is 10110001. Does your result make sense?

slides.title: Hexadecimal numbers
slides.body: Please convert the decimal number 177 to hexadecimal.
What are the ways to represent negative numbers in binary?
Write the (decimal) numbers 77 and −77 as 8-bit signed integers and in 8-bit binary 2's complement. Show your work.

slides.title: Practice: 2's complement
slides.body: Now calculate 110 − 77 in binary 2's complement (that is, perform the addition 110 + (−77)).
slides.note: Reminder: the binary 2's complements of the relevant numbers are 110: 01101110 and −77: 10110011

slides.body: What is the decimal value of the hexadecimal number C3 if binary numbers are represented as:

slides.title: Integer division
slides.body: Recall the funny division behaviour we noticed in the C programming language.

slides.title: Casting an int to a float
slides.body: What do you think happens to the value of the number when you cast an int to a float? Are digits beyond the decimal point added on?
What happens when you similarly cast a float to an int? Will rounding occur?
Suppose you want to know the remainder of the division of one integer by another. Can we do this with our current programming knowledge?

slides.title: Here is what we covered today
items: Converting between decimal and binary numbers
Three ways a binary number can be interpreted: unsigned integer, signed magnitude, binary 2's complement
Hexadecimal numbers as short-hand for binary
Converting (casting) between data types: ave = ((float) a)/((float) b);
Modulo operation to find the remainder in integer division: 8 % 5 = 3
